items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation: I’m plagued by vague misgivings... Shpak has a tape recorder, the envoy has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.justification: The actual translation captures the mock-heroic opening (“vague…”) but renders the elliptical Russian clause with an explicit possession construction (“Shpak has... / the envoy has...”). The approved/reference rendering uses a genitive/nominal shorthand to avoid implying current possession and to preserve the compressed, slightly comic detective-register list. The editional choice in the reference also avoids the possible misreading that these characters currently possess the items (the Russian elliptical form implies the items are notable in relation to them—here, items that are relevant or missing), which is an important nuance in the joke.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Delighted! Very pleased to meet you, Tsar!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.justification: The actual line(s) used in the translation block render a polite, repetitive greeting that preserves the stilted ritual tone. The reference points out a specific interpretive requirement: the gag depends on whether the speaker is introducing himself as 'the Tsar' or addressing a real Tsar. The chosen English line addresses the Tsar ("...meet you, Tsar"), which is linguistically acceptable in context, but it doesn't foreground the meta-joke flagged in the reference (that the impostor is effectively 'introducing himself as the Tsar'). This produces a slight pragmatic ambiguity relative to the reference analysis.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.justification: Translation is equivalent to the approved phrasing and preserves the comic incongruity of a low-level Soviet functionary suddenly issuing a royal command. Matches the reference in register and punch.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.justification: Literal match to the approved translation. The archaic formality and high-register melancholy are preserved exactly as in the reference.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.justification: The actual translation preserves the core exclamation and panic. The reference uses a slightly more explicit subject ("They've walled me in..."), which foregrounds the Tsar's perception (being trapped). The actual is more telegraphic and slightly ambiguous about whether the speaker is addressing the demons or exclaiming about them; this reduces one bit of the original's interpretive clarity but does not fatally alter the joke.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.justification: Both versions convey the Tsar's attributing an apparently miraculous mechanical success to the cross. The reference is more elevated ('Behold the power...'); the actual line is more colloquial ('That’s what... can do!'). The effect—comic juxtaposition of religious conviction and modern machinery—is preserved, but register is slightly flattened compared with the loftier reference.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.justification: The actual translation gives a direct imperative that preserves the invitation to join the chaotic dancing; it is punchy and idiomatic. The reference offers a more idiomatic stage-announcement ('The floor is open to everyone!')—both convey essentially the same performative function. The translation keeps the energy and immediacy, so no substantive issue.

items.translation: What a lucky time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.justification: The actual translation preserves Miloslavsky’s gloating opportunism and the casual, self-satisfied register. It is not identical wording to the reference but matches the reference analysis: a pleased burglar finding a trove of scarce consumer goods. No creative qualities are lost.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... Yes! Overseas caviar... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.justification: The gag rests on juxtaposing luxurious caviar with the mundane Soviet 'eggplant' spread presented as if it were exotic 'overseas' caviar. The actual line preserves the inversion and delivers the punch. The reference cautions translators not to call the eggplant spread 'caviar' (to avoid confusion); the actual keeps the same juxtaposition but still places 'caviar' immediately before 'eggplant,' which could momentarily confuse an unprepared listener. Nonetheless the comic effect survives.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка замшевая - три
items.translation: everything is gone: 3 tape recorders, 3 movie cameras, 3 cigarette cases, 3 domestic cigarette cases, suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.justification: The actual translation reproduces Shpak's comic catalogue of lost/stolen status-items and the accumulating, slightly ridiculous counting. It retains the rhythm and list-build that make the gag land. The phrasing differs but keeps the serial-comedic effect central to the reference analysis.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.justification: The actual translation renders the repeated, resigned formula well; the slight variation in the final clause ('I’ll be cured' vs. 'they’ll cure me') is stylistic and does not change the comedic effect—her resigned, melodious acceptance of 'everyone will be cured.' The tone and melody of the repetition are preserved.

summary.interpretation: 0.91 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.18 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
